name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a Java library in the Machai ecosystem for integrating JVM applications with generative AI providers through a common abstraction for chat-style inference, prompt orchestration, tool calling, optional web search, MCP server connectivity, and embedding generation. The central contracts are org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai for conversational providers and org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider for vector generation. Provider bootstrap is centralized in org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager, which dynamically resolves providers from Provider:Model identifiers such as OpenAI:gpt-4.1, Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet, or CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini. Supported built-in providers include org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider, which adapts the OpenAI Java SDK Responses API and supports prompt accumulation, iterative tool execution, provider-native web search, MCP server registration, usage capture, and embeddings; org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.anthropic.AnthropicProvider, which adapts the Anthropic Java SDK and supports prompts, local tool execution loops, web search, MCP forwarding, configurable timeouts, and token limits; and org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider, which authenticates against an OIDC token endpoint, configures an OpenAI-compatible backend, and delegates to downstream providers according to model family such as gpt-*, claude-*, and gemini-*. Host-side extensibility is provided by org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools and org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader, which use java.util.ServiceLoader to discover and install reusable capability bundles such as file access, command execution, or HTTP retrieval. The Genai API supports initialization, adding prompts, setting instructions, attaching prompt bundles, registering tool bundles, enabling input logging, clearing provider state, and assigning a project directory for tool handlers. This library is suitable for enterprise AI integration, agent-style workflows, retrieval-augmented generation, semantic search, AI-assisted automation, and modular tool-enabled applications that need to switch provider backends through configuration rather than code changes.

usageContext: Java applications that need a provider-neutral API for generative AI requests and embeddings
Systems that must switch between OpenAI, Anthropic, and CodeMie through configuration rather than source changes
Applications that expose local Java functions to LLMs through structured tool-calling workflows
Platforms that need ServiceLoader-based discovery of reusable AI tool bundles
Retrieval-augmented generation, semantic search, similarity ranking, and vector indexing use cases
Enterprise automation scenarios that combine prompts, instructions, local tools, MCP servers, and provider-side web search
Libraries and applications that require configurable timeout, token, logging, and usage-accounting behavior for AI integrations

constructors.description: Creates and initializes a conversational provider from a Provider:Model identifier and a Configurator. This is the main provider-neutral bootstrap entry point for applications that want to select an AI backend at runtime.

examples.description: How to use it: add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.2.0, configure credentials in a Configurator, and resolve a provider with a Provider:Model identifier. This lets the application choose the backend without hard-coding a specific implementation class.
How to use it with CodeMie: configure CodeMie credentials, resolve a CodeMie model, and then use the returned Genai instance through the same API used for OpenAI or Anthropic providers.

constructors.description: Creates and initializes an embedding-capable provider from a Provider:Model identifier. Use this when an application needs vector generation for semantic retrieval, indexing, or similarity search.

examples.description: How to use it: install the library, configure the required provider credential, and resolve an EmbeddingProvider with a Provider:Model string. This is the provider-neutral entry point for embedding workflows.

constructors.description: Constructs a loader that discovers org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools implementations using java.util.ServiceLoader. It is the host-side entry point for installing reusable tool bundles onto a Genai provider.

examples.snippet: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader loader = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
examples.description: How to use it: create the loader during application startup after adding the dependency to the classpath. Then call applyTools on a provider to register all compatible ServiceLoader-discovered tool bundles.

constructors.description: Creates a concrete OpenAI-backed provider implementation. Direct construction is useful when an application intentionally targets OpenAI-specific behavior rather than using provider-neutral bootstrap through GenaiProviderManager.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider provider = new org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider();
provider.init("gpt-4.1", new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator());
examples.description: How to use it: construct the provider directly, initialize it with a model and Configurator, and then call instructions, prompt, and perform. This path is appropriate when the application is intentionally coupled to OpenAI-specific capabilities.

features.name: prompt
features.description: Adds a user prompt to the current provider session. Providers accumulate prompts until perform() is invoked, enabling multi-part conversational request construction.

examples.description: Practical how-to: install the Maven dependency, configure OPENAI_API_KEY, resolve a provider, add an instruction and prompt, and then call perform(). This is the shortest end-to-end scenario for chat-style inference.

features.name: instructions
features.description: Sets system or session instructions for the current conversation. Use it to define behavior, output constraints, tone, or role before prompts are executed.

examples.description: Practical how-to: configure the provider credential, resolve a conversational backend, set stable instructions once, and then send user prompts. This pattern is useful for repeated requests that must follow a consistent style or policy.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes the current request using the configured provider, accumulated prompts, instructions, registered tools, and provider-specific capabilities. Providers may run iterative tool-call loops until a final text answer is produced.

examples.description: Practical how-to: after installation and configuration, prepare the conversation state and call perform(). In tool-enabled workflows, the provider handles intermediate tool invocations before returning the final response text.

features.name: addTool
features.signature: public void org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider.addTool(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String description, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction function, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ParamDescriptor... paramsDesc)
features.description: Registers a local function tool for an OpenAI-backed provider. Parameter descriptors are converted into a JSON Schema object so the model can request structured tool invocations.

examples.description: Step-by-step how to use it: 1) add the library dependency, 2) configure OPENAI_API_KEY, 3) initialize OpenAIProvider, 4) set the project directory if tool execution needs filesystem context, 5) register the tool, 6) prompt the model to use it, and 7) call perform().

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Discovers FunctionTools installers through ServiceLoader, creates fresh instances, filters them by application-class compatibility, and applies them to a Genai provider. This enables modular packaging of reusable host-side tool capabilities.

examples.description: Step-by-step how to use it: 1) install the library dependency, 2) put FunctionTools implementations and META-INF/services registration on the classpath, 3) create and configure the provider, 4) construct FunctionToolsLoader, 5) call applyTools, and 6) execute prompts that can use the discovered tools.

features.name: embedding
features.description: Requests an embedding vector for input text from an OpenAI-compatible model. The returned vector can be used for semantic indexing, similarity search, clustering, ranking, and retrieval pipelines.

examples.description: How to use it: add the dependency, configure OPENAI_API_KEY, resolve an embedding model through GenaiProviderManager, and generate vectors from business or document text for storage in a vector index or similarity engine.

features.name: embedding
features.description: Requests an embedding vector through the CodeMie integration layer. CodeMieProvider first authenticates against the configured OIDC endpoint and then delegates embedding generation to the appropriate downstream provider.

examples.description: How to use it: install the library, provide CodeMie credentials, resolve an embedding provider with a CodeMie model identifier, and call embedding() for retrieval or semantic similarity scenarios in enterprise environments.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
customizations.description: Primary extension contract for conversational provider integrations. Custom implementations can define initialization, prompt handling, instruction management, tool registration, execution, input logging, and project-directory-aware behavior behind a stable API.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement Genai for a new backend, package it in a provider module, and keep application code dependent on the interface rather than a concrete vendor SDK.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
customizations.description: Extension contract for providers that generate embedding vectors. Implement this interface when integrating a new vector-capable backend into the same provider-neutral ecosystem.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement EmbeddingProvider for a new vector backend, initialize it with a model and configuration, and then resolve or use it in semantic search and retrieval workflows.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: SPI for reusable host-side tool bundles. Implementations can contribute curated local capabilities that are discovered through ServiceLoader and applied to providers at runtime.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement the SPI in your application or companion module, register it in META-INF/services, and allow FunctionToolsLoader to discover and apply it automatically.

customizations.name: Provider configuration for web search, MCP, and runtime control
customizations.description: Providers can be customized through configuration keys documented in the Javadocs. Relevant options include OPENAI_API_KEY, OPENAI_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, GENAI_TIMEOUT, MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS, MAX_TOOL_CALLS, WebSearchTool.type, WebSearchTool.city, WebSearchTool.country, WebSearchTool.region, MCP.url, MCP.label, MCP.description, MCP.authorization, numbered MCP groups such as MCP_1.url, and CodeMie-specific AUTH_URL, GENAI_USERNAME, and GENAI_PASSWORD.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: configure credentials, request limits, optional built-in web search, and optional MCP server settings before provider creation so the provider starts with the desired runtime behavior.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Core abstraction for provider-neutral conversational AI workflows, including initialization, prompt collection, instruction management, tool-bundle registration, prompt-bundle registration, state clearing, input logging, execution, and project-directory propagation.

examples.description: How to use it: keep application code dependent on the Genai interface so provider selection remains configuration-driven and backend-neutral.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Abstraction for providers that compute embedding vectors from text. It is intended for semantic indexing, retrieval, similarity comparison, ranking, and vector-search workflows.

examples.description: How to use it: resolve an embedding-capable provider separately from conversational providers when an application only needs vector generation for search or retrieval scenarios.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: SPI contract for modules that contribute reusable host-side tool installers to compatible Genai providers through ServiceLoader discovery.

examples.description: How to use it: implement the interface, register the implementation in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools, and package it on the application classpath for automatic discovery.

examples.title: Install the library as a Maven dependency and execute a basic prompt
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.2.0 to your pom.xml.
Ensure the application runs on Java 8 or newer.
Set the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable or provide the same value through org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator.
Create a Configurator and resolve a provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf).
Call instructions() and prompt() to prepare the request.
Call perform() and consume the returned response text.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.instructions("You are a helpful Java assistant.");
provider.prompt("Explain what a provider abstraction solves in one paragraph.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A practical how-to scenario for first-time adoption. It shows installation, credential configuration, provider creation, prompt submission, and execution using the minimal number of steps.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run CodeMie as the backend provider
steps: Add the genai-client Maven dependency to your application.
Provide GENAI_USERNAME and GENAI_PASSWORD through environment variables or Configurator values.
Optionally set AUTH_URL if your organization uses a non-default CodeMie token endpoint.
Resolve the provider with a CodeMie-prefixed model such as CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini or CodeMie:claude-3-5-sonnet.
Call prompt() and perform() exactly as you would for other providers because the API remains provider-neutral.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider.USERNAME_PROP_NAME, java.lang.System.getenv(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider.USERNAME_PROP_NAME));
conf.set(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider.PASSWORD_PROP_NAME, java.lang.System.getenv(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider.PASSWORD_PROP_NAME));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
provider.prompt("Create a short test plan for a REST API client.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A real-world how-to for organizations that authenticate through EPAM CodeMie and want to keep application code unchanged while selecting different model families through configuration.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run reusable tools through ServiceLoader discovery
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build so provider and tool SPI APIs are available.
Create a FunctionTools implementation in your application or a companion library.
Register that implementation in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools.
Configure the provider with the required API key and create it through GenaiProviderManager.
Construct FunctionToolsLoader and call applyTools(provider, com.example.Application.class).
Prompt the model with a task that can benefit from the installed tools, then call perform().
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

public final class com.example.tools.TimeFunctionTools implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools {
  public void applyTools(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider) {
    // Register tool functions for the provider here.
  }
}

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.setProjectDir(new java.io.File("."));
org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader loader = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
loader.applyTools(provider, com.example.Application.class);
provider.instructions("Use installed tools when necessary.");
provider.prompt("Use an installed tool to inspect the current environment.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A step-by-step how-to that demonstrates installation, tool packaging, ServiceLoader registration, provider setup, tool application, and execution of a realistic tool-enabled workflow.

examples.title: Configure built-in web search and MCP integration before running a prompt
steps: Add the Maven dependency for genai-client.
Set OPENAI_API_KEY in the environment or Configurator.
Configure WebSearchTool.type and optional location properties to enable provider-native web search.
Configure MCP.url, MCP.label, and optional MCP.description and MCP.authorization values to attach an MCP server.
Create the provider through GenaiProviderManager and execute a prompt that may require external context or remote tool support.
examples.description: A configuration-first how-to scenario that shows how to enable external retrieval and MCP-based capabilities without implementing custom provider code.

examples.title: Create embeddings for semantic search and retrieval
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build.
Configure the provider credential for the embedding backend you want to use.
Resolve an embedding provider through GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider(...).
Call embedding(text, dimensions) for each document or query string you want to vectorize.
Store the resulting vectors in a vector database, search index, or in-memory similarity structure.
At query time, generate another embedding and compare it against stored vectors.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider("OpenAI:text-embedding-3-large", conf);
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> documentVector = provider.embedding("Installation guide for the GenAI Client library", 3072L);
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> queryVector = provider.embedding("How do I configure MCP support?", 3072L);
examples.description: A practical how-to for semantic retrieval use cases. It covers installation, provider configuration, vector generation, and the typical document-plus-query workflow used in retrieval-augmented applications.
